requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must maintain High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP) coverage without disqualifying coverage to maximize HSA contributions and leverage triple tax advantage for current and future medical expenses
criteria: Must be covered under qualifying HDHP with minimum deductibles and maximum out-of-pocket limits
2025 HDHP requirements: Minimum deductible $1,650 self-only/$3,300 family; maximum OOP $8,300/$16,600
Cannot have other health coverage except permitted insurance (dental, vision, specific disease, accident insurance)
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare (Part A, B, C, or D) or claimed as dependent on another's tax return
2025 contribution limits: $4,300 self-only, $8,550 family coverage, plus $1,000 catch-up (age 55+)
Contributions can be made by individual, employer, or family members through tax year filing deadline
Last-month rule allows full annual contribution if covered December 1 and maintain HDHP following 12 months
Triple tax benefit: Tax-deductible contributions, tax-free earnings growth, tax-free qualified medical withdrawals

hsa_maximization_strategy.triple_tax_advantage: Deductible contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free withdrawals for qualified medical expenses
hsa_maximization_strategy.investment_opportunity: Can invest HSA funds for long-term growth; no required distribution or use-it-or-lose-it rule
hsa_maximization_strategy.portable_account: Owned by individual, not employer; remains with you regardless of employment changes
hsa_maximization_strategy.retirement_planning: After age 65, can withdraw for non-medical expenses penalty-free (taxable like traditional IRA)
hsa_maximization_strategy.no_rmds: No required minimum distributions unlike retirement accounts; can preserve for legacy planning
hsa_maximization_strategy.family_coverage_advantage: One HSA per individual but family coverage allows higher contribution limit regardless of actual expenses

restrictions: General purpose health FSA disqualifies HSA contributions; must use limited purpose FSA if needed
Spouse's non-HDHP family coverage that covers you disqualifies your HSA contributions
Medicare enrollment (even Part A only) permanently ends HSA contribution eligibility
Excess contributions subject to 6% excise tax annually until corrected; must withdraw excess plus earnings
Last-month rule failure causes income inclusion of improper contributions plus 10% penalty
Non-qualified withdrawals subject to income tax plus 20% penalty (10% penalty after age 65)
California and New Jersey don't conform to federal HSA tax treatment; contributions taxable at state level
Contribution limits reduced if HDHP coverage not maintained all 12 months (unless last-month rule applied)
